d: A silly, frivolous, or excessively chatty person
note: Sounds as chaotic as the person it describes

d: Utterly astonished or struck speechless
note: Gob is British slang for mouth

d: A commotion or fuss caused by conflicting views
note: Soft sounds somehow capturing total disorder

d: Completely exhausted or worn out
note: Too vivid and useful to replace

d: Very pleased or delighted about something
note: Sounds like it should mean annoyed

d: Bitterly disappointed or devastated
note: Same word, opposite emotion across the Atlantic

d: Suspicious, unreliable, or of poor quality
note: British catch-all for anything sketchy

d: Slightly offended or put out by something
note: Mild enough for polite company

d: To repair or construct something badly
note: The British art of fixing things wrong

d: Unsteady, crooked, or malfunctioning
note: Perfectly informal for anything off-kilter

d: To waste time on trivial or ineffectual things
note: Sounds exactly like what it describes

d: To chat idly or at length about nothing
note: Phonetically captures endless chatter

d: Slightly hungry between meals
note: Nobody knows exactly why, but it stuck

d: An exclamation of surprise or alarm
note: Softened form of blind me

d: Insincerely flattering or excessively smug
note: The sound matches the feeling precisely

d: A wealthy or upper-class person
note: Short, sharp, dripping with social commentary

d: An annoying or contemptible person
note: A surprisingly polite insult with real bite

d: Language that is needlessly complex or meaningless
note: Coined by a Texas congressman in 1944

d: A stupid or foolish person
note: Cockney rhyming slang hiding a ruder origin

d: A period of exactly two weeks
note: Standard everywhere except most of North America

d: A stupid or foolish person
note: Made famous by British television

d: Disgusting, ugly, or of very poor quality
note: Scottish origin, spread across the UK

d: Stomach pain or anxiety felt in the abdomen
note: Victorian English for a very recognizable feeling

d: Utter nonsense or complete rubbish
note: Possibly named after a Victorian drinks maker

d: A person who talks at length without making sense
note: Scottish; a word that earns its keep

d: Custom-made or tailored to individual specifications
note: Now global; still quintessentially British

d: Trivial, trifling, or of no real importance
note: Sounds exactly as dismissive as it is

d: To confuse or perplex someone thoroughly
note: American frontier slang, now mostly forgotten

d: To spend time aimlessly or move slowly
note: Sounds playful, means exactly that

d: To leave abruptly or make off quickly
note: 19th-century US slang at its most theatrical

d: To pretend to work while actually doing nothing
note: Invented for a very real human behavior

d: A shrewd, unprincipled person seeking office
note: Mark Twain used it; the language forgot it

d: To speak pompously at length about nothing
note: America invented this and still desperately needs it

d: To argue over trivial or petty points
note: Legal slang gone rogue

d: To move with a clumsy, bouncing gait
note: The sound describes the motion

d: Something ridiculous or curious
note: Victorian slang that never quite caught on

d: To quarrel noisily about trivial things
note: More specific than argue, far less used

d: A political fence-sitter or independent voter
note: 19th-century term still perfectly applicable

d: To leave somewhere hurriedly
note: Civil War era; sounds like the panicked scramble

d: Impudently bold or cheeky in speech
note: A Tudor-era insult deserving revival

d: Having an attractive or handsome face
note: A genuine Tudor compliment, unused since

d: A warning shouted before throwing liquid from a window
note: Medieval Edinburgh's famous and grim civic cry

d: A foolish or stupid person
note: Elizabethan era and still entirely usable

d: A small man with an exaggerated sense of importance
note: Captures the type with unsettling precision

d: To stare at someone eating, hoping to be offered some
note: A precise behavior mostly observed in dogs

d: Moving in a direction contrary to the sun's course
note: Scottish, used in witchcraft and folklore

d: A slobbering, lazy, worthless person
note: 17th century; possibly the greatest English insult

d: Suffering the effects of excessive eating or drinking
note: Sounds rude, is actually medical Latin

d: A person who stubbornly holds a wrong view
note: From a medieval monk who mispronounced Latin mass

d: To make evasive or contradictory statements repeatedly
note: The word for a very common political behavior

d: Giving opinions beyond one's actual knowledge
note: The perfect word for a universal behavior

d: Dingy, unkempt, or having an unpleasant smell
note: Sounds exactly like what it describes

d: One's particular area of interest or expertise
note: Medieval English, surprisingly modern in use

d: The occurrence of events by happy chance
note: Coined by Walpole from a Persian fairy tale in 1754

d: A misheard or misinterpreted word or phrase
note: Coined from a misheard Scottish ballad lyric

d: Something decisive or exceptionally impressive
note: The word Lincoln was hearing when he was shot

d: Cutting sarcasm or snide remarks
note: Lewis Carroll's Snark poem predated this slang use

d: A new term for an old thing, needed after a newer version
note: Acoustic guitar became a retronym after electric ones

d: A state of involuntary romantic obsession and yearning
note: Coined by psychologist Dorothy Tennov in 1979

d: The realization that each stranger has a complex inner life
note: Coined in the Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows

d: Irritatingly self-assertive or overconfident
note: Blend of bump and presumptuous, 19th century

d: Bad-tempered, argumentative, and uncooperative
note: Appeared in the 18th century; origin disputed

d: A bad-tempered, difficult, often elderly person
note: Origin entirely unknown; phonetically perfect

d: To feel nauseous; to move in an unsteady way
note: Middle English; sounds like the motion it describes

d: Of enormous or gigantic size
note: From Swift's Gulliver's Travels; the land of giants

d: Extremely small or trivially unimportant
note: Also from Gulliver; opposite of Brobdingnagian

d: In a state of severe disrepair and rickety
note: Extended from an older word meaning plundered

d: Confused, defeated, or intoxicated
note: Victorian slang of completely unknown origin

d: Sad or miserable in appearance or expression
note: Literally surrounded by woe; the look is in the word

d: Upside down or in complete confusion
note: Reduplication phrase from the 1500s

d: A slow-witted or stupid person
note: 17th-century compound, still satisfyingly precise

d: The act of throwing someone from a window
note: Prague had enough incidents that it needed a word

d: To deceive or trick someone into believing something false
note: 16th-century thieves' slang, still going strong

d: To mix socially with people of a higher social status
note: From 18th-century have or have not

d: Trivial matters; nonsense or foolish talk
note: Reduplication of fiddle, dismissive by design

d: Pleasure felt at the misfortune of others
note: German keeps this concept in one tidy word

d: A strong desire to travel and explore
note: German, because they felt it deeply

d: The defining spirit or mood of an era
note: German, untranslatable and impossible to replace

d: A feeling of deep anxiety or existential dread
note: Norse via German, now universal

d: Art or objects considered tacky yet oddly appealing
note: German-Yiddish for artistic bad taste

d: A ghost that moves objects and causes disturbances
note: German for rumbling ghost

d: A look-alike or supernatural double of a person
note: German for double walker

d: A comprehensive philosophical worldview
note: Too specific to leave untranslated in German

d: Amateur singing to pre-recorded music tracks
note: Japanese portmanteau that conquered the world

d: A massive ocean wave triggered by seismic activity
note: Japanese, because they understood it best

d: A wealthy, powerful business magnate
note: From Japanese taikun meaning great lord

d: A clumsy or accident-prone person
note: Yiddish, because English needed this exact word

d: Audacious self-confidence bordering on shamelessness
note: Yiddish, still irreplaceable in English

d: To chat in a friendly or ingratiating way
note: Yiddish, now standard in business vocabulary

d: A noisy, overexcited reaction to something trivial
note: French; sounds like the chaos it names

d: A feeling of listless dissatisfaction and boredom
note: French because no English word covers it

d: A complete and embarrassing failure or disaster
note: Italian, from Venetian glassblowing slang

d: A long narrow inlet between steep cliffs
note: Norwegian; looks impossible to English readers

d: An official who investigates public complaints
note: Swedish, borrowed entirely without translation

d: Complete nonsense or utter rubbish
note: Dutch dialect for soft excrement, imported politely

d: A social prohibition or ban on certain behavior
note: Tongan, brought to English by Captain Cook

d: A severe hangover; chaotic discordant noise
note: German for the howling of cats

d: Said after someone sneezes; meaning health
note: German borrowed wholesale with no translation

d: A small extra gift given with a purchase
note: Louisiana French via Spanish via Quechua

d: The pleasant smell of earth after rain
note: Coined 1964 from Greek for stone and fluid of gods

d: The ringing or tinkling sound of bells
note: Poe's word; the sound lives inside the spelling

d: Pleasingly smooth and musical to the ear
note: Latin for honey-flowing; sounds exactly like it means

d: Making or characterized by a hissing sound
note: The word itself hisses when you say it

d: The quality of seeming true or believable
note: Six syllables dedicated to the appearance of reality

d: Tending to talk a great deal; very talkative
note: Ironically, a very long word for excessive talkers

d: Having a ready insight or shrewd judgment
note: Often used by people trying to seem perspicacious

d: A soft murmuring or whispering sound
note: The word itself whispers when spoken softly

d: The alignment of three celestial bodies in a line
note: Among the hardest Scrabble words — all odd letters

d: A position where any move makes the situation worse
note: Chess term now used in everyday strategy and politics

d: Words that phonetically imitate the sounds they describe
note: This word about sounds is also one

d: The inability to recall the right word at the right time
note: You know this feeling but probably not the word for it

d: A manuscript page reused after erasing earlier text
note: Now used for anything layered with hidden history

d: Too extreme or great to be expressed in words
note: The word for things that cannot be put into words

d: Having well-shaped buttocks
note: Greek root, clinical look, completely unexpected meaning

d: Given to using very long words
note: A long word that literally means using long words

d: The rumbling sound of gas moving through the intestines
note: Science named it with alarming precision

d: A sequence of dreamlike shifting images
note: Victorian magic lantern shows; now any surreal spectacle

d: Using many words when fewer would suffice
note: Dickens satirized bureaucracy using this very word

d: A tightrope walker
note: Latin for rope walker; sounds far more impressive

d: Bioluminescent plankton that makes ocean water glow
note: Latin for night light

d: Excessively sentimental or maudlin in quality
note: Yiddish from schmaltz meaning rendered fat

d: A short gun with a wide flaring barrel
note: Dutch donderbus meaning thunder gun

d: Exaggerated or uncontrollable emotion or excitement
note: Greek for suffering of the womb — a regrettable origin

d: Placed diagonally; in a state of disorder or disarray
note: American regional slang for anything crooked or awry

d: To confuse or disconcert someone thoroughly
note: Sounds like a joke; became a real word anyway

d: Utterly astonished or overcome with shock
note: Nobody knows exactly where this came from

d: To trick, cheat, or deceive someone
note: 1700s slang of completely unknown origin

d: To cheat or swindle someone
note: American frontier vocabulary for con artists

d: A commotion, fuss, or noisy excitement
note: Rhyming reduplication with no clear etymology

d: Uncontrollably exuberant or boisterous
note: Americanized from the British rumbustious

d: A mischievous person or scamp
note: Originally a Civil War-era political insult

d: Secret or mischievous activity or behavior
note: Possibly from Irish Gaelic for fox tricks

d: Nonsense or meaningless talk
note: Literally pig slop, extended metaphorically

d: Nonsense or insincere talk meant to deceive
note: Irish-American origin, still precise and useful

d: To confuse or perplex someone completely
note: Origin unknown; phonetically perfect

d: A foolish or stupid person
note: 300 years old and nobody knows its origin

d: A young person considered presumptuous or impertinent
note: Historical class anxiety distilled into one word

d: Senseless talk or writing; utter rubbish
note: 16th-century tavern slang for bad mixed drinks

d: An object or gadget whose name cannot be recalled
note: Compound of thing and linguistic surrender

d: A small device or gadget of uncertain name
note: American variant of thingamajig

d: Whether desired or not; in disorganized fashion
note: From Old English will I nill I

d: To waste time by moving or deciding slowly
note: Reduplication of dally, 17th century

d: Reckless and disorganized in behavior
note: Rhyming compound, 17th century

d: Copious but meaningless heated argument
note: Scottish reduplication of argue

d: Excellent or impressive; bold and daring
note: Southern American blend of bold and audacious

d: Something extraordinary or first-rate
note: 19th-century American superlative

d: Ridiculous, implausible, or of poor quality
note: American slang, possibly from decalcomania

d: A mischievous person; a rogue or rascal
note: Extended from rascal, 17th century

d: A word mistakenly used in place of a similar-sounding one
note: Named after the mishearing of acorn as egg corn

d: Placing too much emphasis on trivial details; fussy
note: Scottish origin, spread through American usage

d: A clumsy, slow-witted person
note: 19th-century American, probably from Dutch

d: Stylishly and attractively impressive
note: American slang; sounds exactly as snappy as it means

d: An object or gadget whose name is forgotten
note: Variant of thingamajig, equally helpless

d: A noisy disturbance or commotion
note: 18th century, origin uncertain

d: Courage, determination, and nerve
note: From a brand of bitter medicinal tonic

d: Nonsense or utter foolishness
note: Sounds like what it means

d: Underhand or unscrupulous behavior or trickery
note: Theatrical sounds for theatrical treachery

d: Something remarkable or extraordinary of its kind
note: From Duesenberg automobiles, then American slang

d: At full speed; very quickly
